perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business and trade secret information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, provided that: (i) such information has not been obtained from the Disclosor; (ii) that such possession can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records and (iii) that Recipient so informs Disclosor in writing within 30 days from receipt of such Confidential Information; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business and trade secret information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, regardless of how the information was obtained; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the conditions under which pre-existing information is NOT considered confidential (i.e., it must not have been obtained from the Disclosor and its possession must be demonstrable), the definition of 'Confidential Information' is broadened. This in-text contradiction impacts section 4, which defines the Recipient's obligations regarding the 'Confidential Information', making it unclear whether pre-existing information now falls under those obligations. The change introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of information protected under the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient agrees that the sole purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion. The Recipient will take all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence all Confidential Information and to use such Confidential Information solely for this purpose. Any use by the Recipient of such Confidential Information for any other purpose shall be considered unauthorized.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the conditions under which pre-existing information is excluded. Specifically, the original text excludes information already in the Recipient's possession if (i) it was not obtained from the Disclosor, (ii) its possession is demonstrable, and (iii) the Discloser is informed. The changed text removes these conditions, making any information in the Recipient's possession, regardless of its source, potentially 'Confidential Information'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Recipient will hold 'all Confidential Information' in strictest confidence and use it solely for the Discussion. Because the definition of Confidential Information has been broadened in the Changed Text, there is a direct contradiction with the original purpose/obligations as defined in the Contradicted Text, therefore it is a 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: Where disclosures are made orally or by visual inspection, the Disclosor shall confirm in writing the fact and general nature of each disclosure within a reasonable time after each such disclosure is made.
perturbation.changed_text: Where disclosures are made orally or by visual inspection, the Disclosor is NOT required to confirm in writing the fact and general nature of each disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the Discloser is NOT required to provide written confirmation after oral or visual disclosures, contradicts the existing requirement, creating ambiguity about the procedure for officially acknowledging and recording these disclosures. This in-text contradiction impacts how Section 2 would be interpreted, and how that section can define what confidential information is and isn't.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business and trade secret information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, provided that: (i) such information has not been obtained from the Disclosor; (ii) that such possession can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records and (iii) that Recipient so informs Disclosor in writing within 30 days from receipt of such Confidential Information; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original requirement for the Discloser to provide written confirmation of oral or visual disclosures. This creates an in-text contradiction because Section 2 defines 'Confidential Information' based on disclosures made by the Discloser, but the Changed Text states those disclosures do not need to be confirmed in writing. Section 2(a)(ii) requires 'written records' to demonstrate possession of information. If the Discloser is no longer required to provide written confirmation, it becomes unclear how the Recipient could reliably demonstrate compliance with section 2(a)(ii), and how the Discloser's information is protected. This impacts the contradicted section's ability to define what constitutes confidential information, fitting the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information who are bound in writing to keep it confidential and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may disclose Confidential Information to its employees, agents and representatives, regardless of whether they are bound in writing to keep it confidential, and to any extent deemed necessary by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text now permits the Recipient to disclose confidential information to anyone without requiring a written confidentiality agreement and without limiting the extent of disclosure. This contradicts the original clause, creating an in-text contradiction in Section 6. It introduces legal uncertainty regarding the measures required to protect the Confidential Information and exposes the Discloser to greater risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires that the recipient's employees, agents, and representatives be bound in writing to keep confidential information confidential. The changed text eliminates this requirement, allowing the recipient to disclose confidential information to anyone, regardless of a confidentiality agreement. The contradicted text states that the recipient will not disclose confidential information to others without the discloser's prior written consent. The changed text directly contradicts this by allowing the recipient to disclose confidential information without requiring written consent or a confidentiality agreement, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as it breaks the original compatibility.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall become the shared property of both Discloser and Recipient. The Recipient is not obligated to return any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation changes the ownership of the Confidential Information and removes the obligation to return materials. This directly contradicts the original clause, creating an in-text contradiction in section 8, which concerns rights related to the confidential information. It also undermines the Discloser's control over their information, potentially leading to disputes over ownership and usage rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any information by the Disclosor shall be deemed to constitute by implication or otherwise, a vesting of any title or interest or a grant of any license, immunity or other right to the Recipient with regard to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the Confidential Information becomes shared property and removes the obligation to return materials. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the agreement does not grant any title, interest, or right to the Recipient with regard to the Confidential Information. The change creates a clear and direct conflict regarding ownership and usage rights of the confidential information, which is a strong in-text contradiction because the Discloser is vesting rights in the recipient when the contradicted clause says it isn't.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire agreement and understanding between the parties thereto as to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior discussions, commitments, agreements, arrangements and understandings of any nature between the parties hereto relating to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes a preliminary understanding between the parties and is subject to future negotiations and modifications. It does not supersede any prior agreements or understandings.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the 'entire agreement' clause. This in-text contradiction undermines the validity and enforceability of the entire document, creating significant uncertainty about the parties' obligations. By stating it's a preliminary understanding subject to further negotiation, it introduces an ambiguity which makes previous sections contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of one (1) year from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the agreement was the 'entire agreement' and 'supersedes all prior discussions'. The changed text states it's a 'preliminary understanding' subject to future changes and doesn't supersede prior agreements. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which specifies the agreement comes into force on a specific date and remains in effect for a defined period. If the agreement is preliminary and subject to change, the enforcement date and duration become uncertain, creating a clear in-text contradiction.
